name: Centerfold — Section Drill
description: Drill each section of The J. Geils Band's 'Centerfold'. A driving, high-energy rock song built on simple chord changes. Focus on tight, punchy strumming and keeping up with the tempo.

steps.name: Intro Riff
steps.instructions: The iconic 'na na' riff alternates between G and C. Play tight, muted downstrokes to mimic the staccato keyboard feel. One measure each, keep it punchy.
steps.notes: The original is a keyboard riff, but on guitar you can replicate the energy with short, choppy strums. Try palm muting on the lower strings and letting beats 2 and 4 pop.

steps.name: Verse
steps.instructions: The verse rides on G and C, one measure each. Keep the strumming driving and steady — the vocal rhythm is busy so the guitar needs to be a solid foundation underneath.
steps.notes: 'Does she walk? Does she talk? Does she come complete?' Stay locked into the groove. The G to C change happens every bar — make the transitions snappy with no gaps.

steps.name: Pre-Chorus
steps.instructions: The pre-chorus shifts to Em and C for two measures each, then D for two measures to build tension into the chorus. Let the chords open up here compared to the tight verse strumming.
steps.notes: 'It's okay, I understand...' The Em creates contrast with the G-C verse. The D chord at the end builds anticipation — lean into it and strum harder heading into the chorus.

steps.name: Chorus
steps.instructions: The chorus is the big payoff — G C G C with maximum energy. Open up your strumming, hit all the strings, and drive the rhythm. This is the sing-along moment.
steps.notes: 'My angel is the centerfold!' Same chords as the verse but with more attack. The difference between verse and chorus is all in the dynamics — really dig in here.

steps.name: Outro
steps.instructions: The outro reprises the intro riff over G and C, with the 'na na' hook. Bring back the punchy, staccato feel from the intro. End on a strong G downstrum.
steps.notes: Play through several cycles of the riff, then end cleanly on G. In the original the song fades — in practice, commit to a definitive ending.
